access.provider: University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative

rights.basis: The U-M item carries the RightsStatements.org No Copyright - United States statement.

entities.description: A public 1993 electronic edition structured for U-M's Humanities Text Initiative. Its header names OTA reference U*-243-A and Smith volume III, pp. 66-87. Exact comparison establishes that its 633 verse lines are a mixed-case, structured transformation of the 1984 OSTA/OTA COCOA payload now cataloged as ota:2231, with one normalized line-level difference and added navigation and page structures.
entities.label: University of Michigan electronic item HenOrpheus, OTA U*-243-A
notes: Front matter, back matter, and Smith's editorial notes are explicitly omitted.
The six supplied line-range divisions are electronic navigation units rather than witness structure.

The OTA header's 2000 revision says the Orpheus text formerly belonged to folder 0243 before receiving its own item 2231; this explains the U*-243-A identifier without conflating the 1984 and 1993 encodings.

rights.basis: The U-M item carries the RightsStatements.org No Copyright - United States statement.
rights.component: U-M digital encoding and site presentation

Current LLDS migrated header for OTA item 2231 (malformed)
notes: This current migration derivative is not well-formed XML: authority opens where distributor closes. It also omits metadata preserved in the valid 9,712-byte legacy header, so it is cached for migration-state evidence rather than used as the authoritative catalog header.

localCopies.label: Complete available OTA item 2231 Orpheus COCOA payload

workPortion.label: Complete available 633-line Orpheus payload
locators: COCOA-tagged text after the title and source headings
notes: The header mentions a separate unavailable orpheus2.2231 copy lacking lines 59-175; it is not this payload.

The current LLDS payload is byte-identical to the cached legacy payload. The current LLDS direct header is a shorter, malformed XML migration derivative that omits the legacy header's deposit date, distributor block, and PURL; the valid fuller legacy header is therefore the exact cached source, while both current direct links remain recorded above.

rights.basis: The official OTA item record and TEI header assign Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported to the electronic resource.
rights.component: OTA item 2231 machine-readable header and Orpheus payload
notes: The license applies to the machine-readable resource layer; the medieval work and Smith's 1908 edition are assessed separately.

dateStatements.label: deposit and latest possible digital creation

entities.description: The surviving complete 633-line uppercase COCOA text prepared for the Older Scottish Textual Archive and the Dictionary of the Older Scottish Tongue, deposited with OTA no later than 1984-05-01. Its TEI catalog header names Smith volume III pp. 26-64 as the printed source, describes Chepman-Myllar as the apparent base, and warns that variants may be included. Direct comparison confirms a Chepman-dominant reconstructed stream with Asloan-dominant supply for the unique print copy's lost lines 59-175, Bannatyne-only additions, and local eclectic readings.
entities.label: Older Scottish Textual Archive / Oxford Text Archive COCOA text, ota:2231
notes: Harry D. Watson and the Dictionary of the Older Scottish Tongue are named as depositors; the header separately assigns creation of the machine-readable version to Aitken, Bratley, and Hamilton-Smith.
The header says the text formed part of the Older Scottish Textual Archive and was intended to ensure complete quotation coverage for DOST.

A 2000 revision records that this text moved from folder 0243 to item 2231. It also reports a separate unavailable copy, orpheus2.2231, lacking lines 59-175; that incomplete copy is not the cached or modeled payload.
The available payload retains COCOA page and folio tags at sparse source locations and encodes the full 633-line work.

rights.basis: The official OTA item record and TEI header assign Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported to the electronic resource.
rights.component: OTA item 2231 machine-readable header and Orpheus payload
notes: The license applies to the machine-readable resource layer; the medieval work and Smith's 1908 edition are assessed separately.

bibliographic.citation: The Poems of Robert Henryson, ed. G. Gregory Smith, vol. III, Text vol. II, Scottish Text Society 58 (Edinburgh and London: William Blackwood and Sons, 1908).
bibliographic.pages: Orpheus: Chepman-Myllar pp. 26-64 left; Asloan transcript pp. 27-65 right; Bannatyne pp. 66-87
bibliographic.title: The Poems of Robert Henryson

entities.description: Scottish Text Society 58, volume III overall and text volume II. Smith prints the surviving Chepman-Myllar, Asloan-transcript, and Bannatyne Orpheus streams separately and supplies both the base presentation used by the OSTA/OTA encoding and the Bannatyne page sequence later added by U-M.
entities.label: G. Gregory Smith, The Poems of Robert Henryson, volume III (1908)
notes: Smith thanks George Stevenson for transcribing and collating the texts.

The three streams contain 461 surviving, 578, and 633 lines respectively; the Chepman count reflects two lost leaves in the unique surviving copy, not proof that the original print intentionally omitted lines 59-175.
The Asloan stream is explicitly based on a working transcript lent by Professor Biilbring, not on Smith's direct access to the codex.

rights.basis: First published in 1908, before the United States public-domain cutoff.
rights.component: 1908 editorial text and typography

rights.basis: First published in 1914, before the United States public-domain cutoff.

access.entityLabel: Chepman and Myllar's 1508 print of Orpheus

bibliographic.citation: Heir begynnis the traitie of Orpheus kyng and how he yeid to hewyn and to hel to seik his quene (Edinburgh: Walter Chepman and Androw Myllar, April 1508).
bibliographic.title: Heir begynnis the traitie of Orpheus kyng and how he yeid to hewyn and to hel to seik his quene

entities.description: The first surviving publication, printed at Edinburgh in April 1508. The unique surviving copy is imperfect: the third and fourth leaves of its first gathering are lost, removing unified lines 59-175 and leaving 461 surviving lines. That physical lacuna is not treated as proof of an intentionally shorter printed version. The schema has no historical-print-witness class, so the print is represented as a scholarly-edition entity with that limitation explicit.

historical_designations: eighth tract in The Porteous of Noblenes and ten other rare tracts
holding.notes: The current precise shelfmark was not established from the reviewed public project pages; the surviving collection's leaves were separately mounted in the twentieth century.

entities.label: Chepman and Myllar's 1508 print of Orpheus
notes: The tract's title advertises ‘ane othir ballad in the lattir end.’ That following lyric is The Want of Wise Men, a separate work not part of Orpheus.

The facsimile completely represents the published surviving-image sequence, including the unique copy's lacuna; it does not restore the two lost leaves or constitute a complete copy of the original printed tract.

rights.basis: The print was published in 1508.
rights.component: 1508 printed text and historical object

access.accessMethod: Direct Wikimedia Commons DjVu download; NLS also supplies an official page viewer
access.entityLabel: Complete 220-image public derivative of the Chepman and Myllar prints

localCopies.label: Chepman and Myllar Prints complete 220-image published sequence

workPortion.label: Surviving leaves of the imperfect unique Chepman-Myllar Orpheus tract
notes: Image 150 is the title verso and bears a manuscript ‘Orpheus’; printed poem text begins on image 151.
Image 152 ends at line 58; image 153 resumes at unified line 176 because the third and fourth leaves of the first gathering, which carried lines 59-175, are lost from the unique copy.
Image 166 ends ‘And thus endis the tale of Orpheus’ and then begins the separate Want of Wise Men on the same surface.
Complete here means all 220 published facsimile images in this derivative, not a materially complete copy of the Orpheus tract or unrepresented physical covers and flyleaves.

rights.basis: The Commons file page identifies the reproduction as public domain; the underlying 1508 print is independently public domain.

next_steps: Seek OSTA/DOST operator instructions or production notes and complete a variant concordance across the OTA payload and all three Smith streams.
notes: The direct 1984 OTA-to-1993 U-M chain and broad source pattern are resolved: Chepman dominates, Asloan mainly supplies the lost print leaves, and Bannatyne supplies its unique additions. Only the rule for local variant choices remains open.
openQuestions.question: What exact OSTA/DOST editorial rule governed choices among Smith's three streams in the available orpheus1.2231 payload, especially its local hybrid readings?

next_steps: Search Smith and Biilbring papers, Scottish Text Society archives, Bonn holdings, and NLS acquisition files before assigning a location or declaring the transcript lost.
openQuestions.question: Does Professor Biilbring's working transcript of the Asloan Orpheus survive, and if so under what shelfmark or archival description?

next_steps: Review the stemmatic argument and variants in current textual scholarship; do not add a direct derivation edge while the relationship remains only a possibility.
openQuestions.question: Does the Asloan Orpheus depend directly on the 1508 Chepman-Myllar print?

next_steps: Ask NLS rare-books staff or consult the current rare-books catalog before citing a present shelfmark.
openQuestions.question: What is the current precise NLS shelfmark for the mounted Chepman and Myllar print collection formerly designated Advocates Library 19.1.16?

reviewNotes: The intellectual work includes both the 414-line tale and 219-line Moralitas. The Want of Wise Men is a separate work despite its placement and advertisement in the 1508 tract.

Chepman-Myllar, Asloan, and Bannatyne are three textual streams with 461 surviving, 578, and 633 lines. The unique Chepman-Myllar copy has lost the two leaves that carried unified lines 59-175; its surviving count is not modeled as an intentionally shorter complete version.
Professor Biilbring's transcript is retained as an explicit intermediary between Asloan and Smith; Smith is not falsely linked as a direct transcriber of the inaccessible codex.

The source chain is explicit: the repository XML copies U-M's 1993 U*-243-A item, which transforms the available 1984 OSTA/OTA item 2231 payload, which in turn cites and combines Smith's separately printed streams. The OTA and U-M stages align for all 633 verse lines, with 632 exact normalized matches and only BUS versus buss at line 98.

Sixteen exact source files are cached: complete 220-image Chepman, 628-canvas Asloan, and 454-canvas Bannatyne bundles; Smith volume I; the incomplete Internet Archive Smith volume III derivative plus its missing printed page 37 from Google Books; separate legacy and current-migration OTA headers, the complete available OTA payload; and the complete METS PDF. Exact work ranges, shared boundary surfaces, working direct image links, byte counts, and hashes remain visible.
Boethius and Nicholas Trivet are intellectual sources modeled in the descriptive metadata. No surviving physical exemplar is invented in the lineage graph.
Kindrick 1997 is a later collateral comparison edition. Its official PDF is locally cached for research but remains copyrighted and is not an upstream source of the 1993 encoding.

Access, local caching, underlying-work copyright, edition copyright, image rights statements, and provider terms are separate assertions. A downloaded file is not treated as automatically redistributable.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a copy of the 1993 University of Michigan item HenOrpheus, OTA U*-243-A. Its line text descends from the surviving 1984 Older Scottish Textual Archive / Oxford Text Archive COCOA payload now cataloged as ota:2231 and formerly held in OTA folder 0243: after headings and markup are removed, 632 of 633 normalized verse lines match, with only line 98 changing from BUS to buss. OTA's header credits A. J. Aitken, P. Bratley, and N. Hamilton-Smith with creating the machine-readable version, cites G. Gregory Smith's 1908 volume III pp. 26-64 as its printed base, and warns that variants may be included. Smith prints a Chepman-Myllar stream with 461 surviving lines from an imperfect unique copy, a 578-line Asloan stream mediated by Professor Biilbring's working transcript, and the 633-line Bannatyne stream. Comparison shows that the OTA text is Chepman-dominant, supplies the lost Chepman lines 59-175 mainly from Asloan with local eclectic readings, and takes Bannatyne-only additions at 509-514, 547-550, and 571-615. U-M then converted the uppercase COCOA text into structured mixed-case XML, supplied navigation divisions and Smith's Bannatyne-stream page milestones 66-87, and omitted Smith's editorial matter. This record keeps the medieval work, witnesses, transcript intermediary, scholarly editions, two electronic stages, repository artifact, and source objects distinct.

catalogSummary.author: Robert Henryson
catalogSummary.date: Late fifteenth century; c. 1480 is a conventional approximation, not a secure exact date
catalogSummary.editor: No editor at work level; G. Gregory Smith's 1908 parallel edition underlies the 1984 OSTA/OTA machine-readable text later transformed at U-M
catalogSummary.region: Scotland; mythic action ranges from Thrace through the planetary spheres to the classical underworld
catalogSummary.title: Orpheus and Eurydice

catalogingSubject.label: Orpheus and Eurydice
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Robert Henryson's late-fifteenth-century Older Scots poem, including its narrative tale (lines 1-414) and its integral Moralitas (lines 415-633), as one intellectual work. The imperfect unique Chepman-Myllar print copy, the 578-line Asloan and 633-line Bannatyne witnesses, Smith's parallel edition, the 1984 OSTA/OTA machine-readable text, the 1993 U-M transformation, later critical editions, and facsimiles are transmission layers rather than separate works. The separate lyric The Want of Wise Men, printed after Orpheus in the 1508 tract and advertised as ‘ane othir ballad,’ is outside this record.

contentParts.label: Orpheus and Eurydice
contentParts.summary: The single counted poem, comprising the tale and its integral Moralitas.

contentParts.label: The tale of Orpheus and Eurydice
contentParts.summary: The romance narrative from Orpheus's genealogy and marriage through Eurydice's death, the cosmic and infernal quest, her conditional release, and her loss when Orpheus looks back.

contentParts.label: Moralitas Fabule Sequitur
notes: The 219 verse lines are a structural metric, not 219 separately counted items. The source XML does not group the Moralitas into LG stanza containers.
contentParts.summary: An allegorical exposition mapping the myth's characters, journey, monsters, punishments, and backward glance onto intellect, appetite, sin, worldly instability, eloquence, and contemplation.

dateStatements.display: Late fifteenth century; c. 1480 is a conventional or legacy approximation
notes: The repository XML supplies c. 1480 without an evidentiary explanation. The broad range records the late-fifteenth-century scholarly context without converting that legacy value into a secure date.

dateStatements.display: April 1508, in Walter Chepman and Androw Myllar's Edinburgh print collection
notes: The unique surviving 1508 copy is imperfect: the third and fourth leaves of its first gathering, carrying unified lines 59-175, are lost. Its 461 surviving lines do not establish that the original print was intentionally a shorter version. Bannatyne is the first surviving witness with all 633 lines.

extent.description: One counted verse work in two stable conceptual parts. Lines 1-414 form 57 stanza groups; lines 415-633 form a continuous 219-line Moralitas. Verse groups and lines are structural metrics, not separately counted works or items. The repository XML's six supplied line-range DIV2 units are navigation subdivisions, not treated as authorial sections.

formStatements.description: A 633-line verse work. The tale has 57 stanzas: fifty-two seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas and five ten-line lyric stanzas; the 219-line Moralitas is encoded as continuous verse rather than LG stanza groups.

languageStatements.label: Older Scots
notes: Often described more specifically as Middle Scots. The brief Latin Moralitas rubric and learned names do not make the poem as a whole multilingual.
The six supplied line-range DIV2 headings in HenOrpheus.xml are electronic navigation units, not treated as a stable authorial hierarchy.

Witness spelling, the unique Chepman copy's physical lacuna, scribal and print features, Smith's collation, and the OSTA/OTA-to-U-M transformation remain in the lineage manifest rather than being collapsed into work-level metadata.
Boethius and Nicholas Trivet are modeled as intellectual source works without asserting a particular surviving manuscript or edition as Henryson's physical exemplar.

next_steps: Consult current work-specific chronology and textual scholarship before narrowing the normalized range or naming a place of composition.
openQuestions.question: Can Orpheus and Eurydice be dated or localized more narrowly than late-fifteenth-century Scotland without converting literary inference or Henryson's Dunfermline association into documentary fact?

notes: This describes the poem's literary and linguistic context, not a documented exact place of composition.
Henryson is documented in Dunfermline in 1477-1478. That biographical association does not prove the poem was composed there.
A mythological setting rather than a claim about historical geography.
The Bannatyne copy ends ‘Finis: quod mr. R. H.’; current scholarship and catalogs accept the attribution to Henryson. The anonymous 1508 print does not by itself name him.

summaries.value: Henryson recounts Orpheus's lineage, marriage to Eurydice, and grief after she is taken to the underworld. Orpheus searches the planetary spheres, learns celestial harmony, descends through hell, charms its guardians and tormented figures, and wins Eurydice's release on condition that he not look back; his backward glance loses her again. A long Moralitas then interprets Orpheus as the intellectual or reasoning power of the soul and Eurydice as affection or appetite, turning the classical romance into instruction about sin, eloquence, worldly desire, contemplation, and the discipline of reason.

This record describes one 633-line intellectual work, including its tale and Moralitas. It does not describe the separate Want of Wise Men printed immediately afterward in 1508, nor does it conflate the imperfect surviving Chepman print, the Asloan and Bannatyne witnesses, Smith's 1908 parallel presentation, the 1984 OSTA/OTA COCOA text, the 1993 U-M transformation, or modern facsimiles with the work itself.

notes: A normalized conventional title used for human-readable discovery; surviving witnesses vary the spelling of Eurydice's name.
Discovery title of the U-M/CME item, not an independently transmitted medieval title.
The 1508 print continues ‘and ane othir ballad in the lattir end’; that phrase advertises a separate following lyric and is excluded from the work title here.
titles.value: Heir begynnis the traitie of Orpheus kyng and how he yeid to hewyn and to hel to seik his quene

notes: Smith reports this early descriptive title in a Cambridge note associated with Gavin Douglas; it is retained as historical reception evidence, not preferred over the established title.

work.summary: Henryson recounts Orpheus's lineage, marriage to Eurydice, and grief after she is taken to the underworld. Orpheus searches the planetary spheres, learns celestial harmony, descends through hell, charms its guardians and tormented figures, and wins Eurydice's release on condition that he not look back; his backward glance loses her again. A long Moralitas then interprets Orpheus as the intellectual or reasoning power of the soul and Eurydice as affection or appetite, turning the classical romance into instruction about sin, eloquence, worldly desire, contemplation, and the discipline of reason.
